A reasons why, like it will depend on whether or not they've like already used other types of tools that are kind of similar to us or whether or not their team is kind of now being like forced to rethink how they do their process.

For those that haven't, let's say, used too many design tools or AI tools, we normally find people will reach out because A, they're getting like a lot of like top-down pressure to kind of rethink their strategy or just like to rethink how they can go to market faster.

I think the other reasons why they would come to us are like largely due to like referral is like a really big one when folks, like designers will talk to other designers and like, oh, like how did you solve this problem?
Like I think the vast majority of our larger deals have come either through referral or either through like the PLG self-discovery funnel.
So those are like the main ones.
I would say.
For those that have used AI tools, often they'll come to us because we are just literally, if they're designing for the real world, we're trained on that type of context.
And we're also one of the only tools that allow for more multimodal input, not just like you can export it to whatever version you want.
So what I mean by that is like a lot of tools require you to like upload a graphic or like to use a prompt to get started.

So it's not stuck in this like, linear flow of like, okay, cool, now adjust this thing.
Okay, now do that.

And whatever real-world examples you have, whenever you need.

I think like the biggest one is honestly like security and ownership.

Um,

Within different industries or within different sides of companies, or even just different roles, like concept artists, who I think are actually like the most disrupted by like AI and design, are like unbelievably anti-AI.
Whereas you have folks that are in more of like the industrial or like architectural side of things, they're like, absolutely, like eliminate the work that I don't care about.
Like, and so I think that's a big one.

The, even just like going deeper onto like the differences in between security and ownership there, like the security side is literally like, if you are working with New Balance and Nike and also Adidas, like, are they going to see my ?
Are we all training on the same thing?
Like, that's really important.
And also just like AI, um, uh, ethics comes up a ton on, um, are we housing them in a certain way?
Like, are we, um, how do we be.
Purge data, like, what does that look like?
From an ownership standpoint, that's typically more from, like, a designer side, not just kind of what I just mentioned from security, but also, like, do you own the designs that we create in vizcom?
Are you able to use those at your own will, or do I own them as a designer?
What's your guys' answer to that?
I guess let's start with security.
Like, is there a zero data retention policy, or do you guys train on generations?
We do not train on their generations.
We have kind of our own proprietary, and we have an AI team that works through a lot of those.
We also, like, do keep them separate, as your workspace is isolated to within your workspace.
So your workspace can learn from other things within your workspace, unless you, like, disable that, with the assumption of being, like, your team is the one that works within your workspace.
So, like, obviously you want to, like, learn from each other's work.
But outside of that, like, no, it doesn't go into, like, our broader model.
And I think, like, the area that's, like, a little bit fuzzier for us today is, like, we don't necessarily allow folks to, like, toggle which models are working on in the back end for them.
So, like, there are certain customers that will come in and be like, oh, like, I don't want to touch Nana Banana.

And so that's, like, the manual thing today, whereas, like, there isn't necessarily, like, a setting that they can go in and just, like, say, I don't want, like, this Chinese model working with us or things like that, which does come up.
